original_hypothesis.title: Duplicate Holder Array Entries via Zero-Value Transfers Enable Reward Theft and Permanent Distribution DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Any address (even unapproved, with zero balance) can call transfer(approvedAddress, 0) repeatedly. In _beforeTokenTransfer, balanceOf(to) is checked BEFORE the transfer executes. Since the target has 0 balance and receives 0 tokens, balanceOf(to)==0 on every call, causing holders.push(to) on every call. This creates unbounded duplicate entries in the holders array. During distribution, each duplicate entry triggers a separate entitlement calculation (erc20EntitlementPerUnit * balanceOf(address)), causing the same address to receive N times their fair share. When total distributions exceed the contract's token balance, the ERC20 transfer reverts, permanently locking the contract in distribution state (LockedForDistribution=true), blocking all transfers, mints, and burns forever.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a compound code defect: (1) _beforeTokenTransfer checks balance pre-transfer allowing repeated 0-value push, (2) no duplicate check on holders.push, (3) no minimum transfer amount. The distribution loop iterates over all entries including duplicates, computing full entitlement for each. Any external address can trigger this without tokens or approval.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0); if (!exists) { holders.push(to); } — checked pre-transfer, no dedup, allows 0-amount transfers to push duplicates

preconditions: At least one approved holder address exists with zero balance
No minimum transfer amount enforced (confirmed: OZ ERC20 allows 0-value transfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: entitlement = erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] * this.balanceOf(recipient) computed per holders array entry including duplicates
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) N times → N entries for same address → N * entitlement per distribution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If target address has balance B and appears N times, they receive N * (balance/supply) * B instead of (balance/supply) * B. Net theft = (N-1) * fair_share.

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per 0-value transfer to inflate holders?
How many duplicates needed to drain entire reward pool in one distribution?
Does the distribution permanently lock when transfers revert due to insufficient balance?
validation_requirements: Confirm OZ ERC20 0.8.12 allows 0-value transfers without revert
Test that unapproved addresses can call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) successfully
Verify permanent lock when distribute() reverts mid-execution on insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of distributable reward pool per cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI (zero capital, full reward theft)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions to build duplicates, then single distribution block

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% reward loss if DoS locks contract permanently
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All distributable ERC20 balances + permanent protocol freeze

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: holders array growth rate vs gas limits, distribution gas per entry
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify N*entitlement > contract balance triggers permanent lock

validation_result.mechanism: Zero-value transfer duplicate holder array inflation
validation_result.impact_summary: Any address can inflate the holders array with unlimited duplicate entries for an approved zero-balance address via repeated zero-value transfers. Once that address obtains a balance, distribution pays N times their fair share, causing insolvency and permanent distribution lock (DoS).
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability is a compound defect in _beforeTokenTransfer. It checks `this.balanceOf(to) != 0` BEFORE the ERC20 core updates balances, and since a zero-value transfer adds 0 tokens, the target's balance remains 0 across all calls. Each call pushes `to` into the `holders` array without any deduplication. The `_afterTokenTransfer` hook only attempts to remove `from` (the sender), not `to` (the receiver), so the duplicate entries for `to` persist. OpenZeppelin's ERC20._transfer requires `fromBalance >= amount`, and `0 >= 0` is always true, so any address with zero balance can call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) successfully. The sender does not need to be an approved holder—the allowlist check in _beforeTokenTransfer only validates the `to` address. During distribution, each duplicate entry computes `entitlement = erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] * this.balanceOf(recipient)`, and since `balanceOf` returns the same non-zero value for all N entries, the recipient receives N times their fair share. When total obligations exceed the contract's distributable ERC20 balance, the standard ERC20 transfer reverts, causing the entire distribute() call to revert while LockedForDistribution remains true, permanently freezing all transfers, mints, and burns.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 127-140 of LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol contain the defective _beforeTokenTransfer hook. Line 137 (`bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0)`) checks balance pre-transfer; line 139 (`holders.push(to)`) adds duplicates unconditionally. The distribution loop at lines 198-215 iterates every holders entry and computes full entitlement per entry. No minimum transfer amount, no deduplication guard, and no membership set tracking exists anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: balanceOf(to) is checked before the ERC20 balance update. For zero-value transfers, the target balance never changes from 0, so `exists` is always false and `holders.push(to)` executes on every call. No deduplication check prevents the same address from being pushed multiple times.

Each duplicate entry in holders triggers a full entitlement calculation and transfer. With N duplicate entries for an address holding B tokens, total distributed = N * (erc20EntitlementPerUnit * B), which can exceed the contract's balance causing a revert that permanently locks distribution.

Only attempts to remove `from` (sender), never `to` (receiver). When the attacker's address differs from the target, the attacker is never in the holders array, so nothing is removed. The duplicate `to` entries persist.

calling_sequence: 3. _beforeTokenTransfer(attacker, approvedHolder, 0): LockedForDistribution is false ✓, approvedHolder is approved ✓, neither from nor to is address(0) so no mint/burn check, balanceOf(approvedHolder) == 0 → exists = false → holders.push(approvedHolder)
5. _afterTokenTransfer(attacker, approvedHolder, 0): balanceOf(attacker) == 0 → tries to remove attacker from holders → attacker not in array → no-op
6. Repeat steps 1-5 N times: holders array now has N entries of approvedHolder
7. Owner mints tokens to approvedHolder: _beforeTokenTransfer adds yet another entry (N+1)
8. When distribution triggers: each of N+1 entries computes entitlement = erc20EntitlementPerUnit * balanceOf(approvedHolder) = erc20EntitlementPerUnit * B
9. After a few successful transfers exhaust the distributable balance, subsequent transfers revert, permanently locking the contract
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: transfer() → ERC20._transfer() → _beforeTokenTransfer() line 137-139: balanceOf(to)==0 check pre-transfer + holders.push(to) without dedup → distribute() lines 206-210: entitlement computed per duplicate entry → revert on insufficient balance → LockedForDistribution stays true permanently

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script (contract-deployer.ts) deploys with MinDistributionPeriod=10 and empty initial holders/managedNFTs arrays. No configuration prevents this vulnerability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is fully validated: (1) Any EOA with 0 balance can call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) because OZ ERC20 allows 0-value transfers and the sender doesn't need allowlist approval. (2) Each call adds a duplicate entry because balanceOf is checked pre-transfer and 0+0=0. (3) After the target receives tokens via mint or transfer from another holder, distribution computes full entitlement per duplicate. (4) Standard ERC20 transfer reverts on insufficient balance, causing permanent lock. The attack cost is gas-only (~21k+ gas per duplicate entry) with zero capital requirement.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: Any external account can permanently brick the entire protocol by inflating the holders array. The attack requires zero tokens, zero approval, and costs only gas. Impact includes: (1) Reward theft - the duplicated holder receives N times their fair share before insolvency. (2) Permanent DoS - once distribution encounters insufficient balance, LockedForDistribution stays true forever, blocking ALL transfers, mints, and burns. (3) Total loss of all distributable ERC20 balances held by the contract.
recommended_mitigations: Use a mapping (address => bool) to track membership in the holders array, preventing duplicate entries: `mapping(address => bool) private isHolder;` checked before push
Add address(0) check before pushing to holders: `if (to != address(0) && !isHolder[to] && this.balanceOf(to) == 0)` with `isHolder[to] = true`
Use SafeERC20 for all distributable token transfers to handle non-standard return values
Add a recovery mechanism to _endDistribution that can be triggered by the owner if distribution is stuck
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with MinDistributionPeriod=1 and one distributable ERC20. 2. Owner approves holderA. 3. From random account X (0 balance, unapproved), call transfer(holderA, 0) 10 times. 4. Verify holders.length == 10. 5. Owner mints 100 tokens to holderA (adds 11th entry). 6. Transfer distributable ERC20 to the contract. 7. Mine MinDistributionPeriod blocks. 8. Call distribute(11). 9. Observe that the first distribution succeeds (draining the pool), and subsequent entries cause revert. 10. Verify LockedForDistribution is permanently true and all transfers are blocked.
uncertainties: Exact gas cost profile for large-scale holder array inflation (may vary by chain/EVM implementation)

Whether partial distribute() calls could advance past the failing entries by catching reverts - they cannot in Solidity 0.8.x without try/catch on external calls (the transfer is external but reverts propagate)
additional_testing_needed: Measure maximum holders array inflation rate within a single block's gas limit
Test interaction between this bug and the address(0) burn bug (INV-STATE-001) for compounding effects
Verify behavior with non-reverting ERC20s (tokens returning false) - the if-check would skip but the holder still receives overpayment from earlier entries
